events.label: reasoning
events.text:
We need answer JSON only. Need assess scenario: backstabbed device on 20A circuit. TRD says legal but journeymen won't. Need decide compliance per NEC 110.14. Need determine if fail/pass/needs more info. Let's reason.

NEC 110.14 Electrical Connections. Requires terminals and splices suitable for use, torque, etc. Backstab (push-in) terminals on receptacles/switches are permitted by UL listing and NEC if used within listing (e.g., 14 AWG only typically; some allow 12 AWG? Modern back-wires with clamp are okay; old push-in backstab often rated 15A, 14 AWG). On 20A circuit, if device is backstabbed with 12 AWG? Need know. The scenario says "backstabbed device on 20A circuit (TRD: legal but journeymen won't)." It implies backstab connection is legal but disfavored. NEC 110.14 requires connections to be made with approved connectors and according to manufacturer instructions. Backstab terminals are listed and legal if used per listing. However, on 20A circuit, if the device backstab terminal is only rated for 15A or 14 AWG, then non-compliant. But many 20A receptacles have back-wire clamps, not backstabs. The phrase "backstabbed device on 20A circuit" suggests a push-in backstab on a 20A circuit. Is that compliant? Let's recall NEC: Receptacles rated 15A can be used on 20A branch circuits (210.21(B)(3)), but the terminals must be suitable. Backstab terminals on 15A receptacles are typically only for 14 AWG. On a 20A circuit, conductor must be 12 AWG (unless tap). If backstab accepts only 14 AWG, then using 12 AWG in backstab is not per listing and violates 110.14. Also push-in terminals on 15A devices are not rated for 20A? The receptacle itself is 15A but allowed on 20A circuit. The connection method must be suitable. Backstab may be listed for 15A receptacle with 14 AWG. If circuit is 20A with 12 AWG, backstab not suitable. So likely non-compliant. But the TRD note says "legal but journeymen won't" maybe meaning backstabs are legal in general but not best practice. On 20A circuit, legal? Need parse.

The task: "observed field condition is: backstabbed device on 20A circuit (TRD: legal but journeymen won't)." We need decide whether work meets applicable code. Reference NEC 110.14. We need apply rather than quote mechanically.

NEC 110.14(A) Terminals: Connection of conductors to terminal parts shall ensure a thoroughly good connection without damaging the conductors and shall be made by means of pressure connectors (including set-screw type), solder lugs, or splices to flexible leads. Connection by means of wire-binding screws or studs and nuts having upturned lugs or the equivalent shall be permitted. Terminals for more than one conductor and terminals for connecting aluminum shall be so identified.

110.14(B) Splices: Conductors shall be spliced or joined with splicing devices identified for the use or by brazing, welding, or soldering with a fusible metal or alloy. Soldered splices shall first be spliced or joined to be mechanically and electrically secure without solder and then soldered. All splices and joints and the free ends of conductors shall be covered with an insulation equivalent to that of the conductors or with an identified insulating device. Wire connectors or splicing means and insulation for use with conductors shall be listed.

Backstab push-in terminals are listed pressure connectors. If used per listing, compliant. The issue is 20A circuit. If device is backstabbed, is the terminal listed for 20A and 12 AWG? Many backstab terminals on 15A devices are only for 14 AWG. If the installer backstabbed 12 AWG into a 15A device, that's a violation of listing and 110.14. Also 20A circuit requires 12 AWG conductors (NEC 210.19(A)(4) etc). So likely fail.

But the scenario may be intentionally ambiguous: "TRD: legal but journeymen won't" suggests the technical reference document says legal but journeymen won't do it. Maybe the correct decision is pass because backstab is legal if listed, even if journeymen avoid. But…
